Article.ArticleTitle: Are iron and manganese key factors in nitrate attenuation within the shallow groundwater of the nitrogen attenuation zone?

Abstract.AbstractText: Nitrogen attenuation zone (NAZ), characterized by high surface total soil nitrogen (TSN) and groundwater intrinsic vulnerability (Inv) but low groundwater total nitrogen (TN) concentrations, were identified in a regional shallow groundwater nitrogen pollution study. The relatively high Fe2+(1.50 mg/L) and Mn2+(0.35 mg/L) concentrations in these zones prompted the hypothesis that they might participate in the nitrate nitrogen (NO3-N) reduction within nutrient-deficient groundwater. However, the occurrence and contribution of this process remained unclear. Consequently, five boreholes were sampled in the southeastern suburb of Beijing, a typical NAZ in the North China Plain. The results indicated that the coexistence of high TSN and low groundwater TN concentrations was primarily due to NO3-N attenuation during infiltration, with silty clay in the vadose zone and the water table fluctuation zone (WTF zone) serving as crucial attenuation layers. Enzyme activity, NO3-N reduction capacity, and microbial communities and genes analysis revealed that in boreholes where NO3-N infiltrated into groundwater, Fe(II) and Mn(II) in the low-DOC WTF zone may serve as primary electron donors for NO3-N reduction. Combined with the observation that Fe(II)-Mn(II) autotrophic denitrifying bacteria are key modules for NO3-N attenuation, indicating that Fe(II) and Mn(II) are currently crucial to NO3-N attenuation in the WTF zone, especially in low-DOC and high Fe(II)-Mn(II) strata. Incubation experiments revealed that in the low-DOC, high Fe(II)-Mn(II) WTF zone, the contributions of Fe(II), DOC, S2-, and Mn(II) to NO3-N reduction were 88.35 %, 9.15 %, 2.10 %, and 0.40 %, respectively, and when DOC/NO3-N < 0.7 and DOC/Fe(II)<0.023, Fe(II) tended to become the primary electron donor. These findings revealed the dominant role of Fe(II) in NO3-N reduction, rather than Mn(II), likely due to the significantly lower Mn(II) (44.17 mg/kg) concentration in sediments than Fe(II) (4021.59 mg/kg). Further electron balance analysis of key nitrogen attenuation zones across five profiles revealed that DOC and Fe(II) were the predominant electron donors for NO3-N reduction in the vadose zone and WTF zone, accounting for 51.44-92.98 % and 27.4-52.43 %, respectively, further confirming that Fe(II) was the key factor for NO3-N attenuation in NAZs. Future studies could provide a basis for differentiated fertilizer nitrogen regulation by dividing NAZs and exploring nitrogen loading thresholds supported by the nitrogen attenuation capacity of different NAZs.
